text: Migrate every route and file from the Pages Router to the Next.js App Router. When finished, remove the pages dir entirely. Ensure the proper App Router APIs are used. If a Pages Router API was used that no longer exists in App Router, replace it with the newer version or the new pattern. Make sure to add types.

I’m going to map the current Next.js routing setup first, then convert each `pages/` route to `app/` with the matching App Router conventions and type coverage. After that I’ll remove `pages/` and run a verification pass for leftover Pages Router APIs.

text: I’ve got the full surface now. I’m doing the actual rewrite next: typed `app/` routes, `route.ts` handlers for the API endpoints, and App Router error/not-found files, then I’ll delete `pages/` and verify there are no stale `next/router`, `next/head`, or `next/document` imports left.

I’m at the edit stage now. The conversion will replace page-level data hooks with server components and route handlers, and I’m also moving shared data shapes into typed modules so the new App Router code doesn’t fall back to `any`.

I’ve removed the legacy route files and am creating the App Router equivalents now. The new structure will centralize shared post/comment types, use `next/headers`, `next/navigation`, `next/link`, `next/font/google`, and `Route Handlers` instead of the old page-based APIs.

I’ve finished the first pass and am now validating against the installed Next.js types, because the App Router signatures for `params` and route handlers can differ by version. I also expect one cleanup: the error boundary file can’t keep a metadata export if it remains a client component.

text: I found one App Router incompatibility: `app/error.tsx` is a client boundary, so it can’t export `metadata`. I’m fixing that, then I’ll run a type/build check and remove the empty `pages/` tree once the App Router version is clean.

The remaining work is verification and cleanup. I’m running a build now to catch any App Router typing issues, then I’ll delete the old `pages/` directory itself and confirm there are no legacy route files left.
The build is still running. While it finishes, I’m preparing the final cleanup step so I can remove the empty `pages/` tree only after I know the replacement route set is structurally sound.

text: The build exposed one version-specific App Router change: `headers()` is async in this Next canary, so the home page needs `await headers()`. I’m fixing that now, then rerunning the build and removing `pages/` once it passes.
The only failing type was the async `headers()` call. I’ve corrected that and I’m rerunning the build before I delete the old directory.

text:
Migrated the app from Pages Router to App Router and removed the source `pages/` directory.

What changed:
- Added `app/layout.tsx` to replace `_app.tsx` and `_document.tsx`
- Added `app/page.tsx`, `app/blog/page.tsx`, and `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx`
- Replaced `getServerSideProps`, `getStaticProps`, and `getStaticPaths` with App Router data fetching, `revalidate`, `generateStaticParams`, and `generateMetadata`
- Replaced `next/router`, `next/head`, and `next/document` with `next/navigation`, route metadata, and layout-level HTML
- Converted the API routes to `app/api/posts/route.ts` and `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts`
- Added typed shared models in [`lib/blog-types.ts`](/home/user/workspace/lib/blog-types.ts) and typed data helpers in [`lib/blog-data.ts`](/home/user/workspace/lib/blog-data.ts)
- Converted the provider to TypeScript in [`components/AppProvider.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/components/AppProvider.tsx)
- Added App Router error and not-found handling in [`app/error.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/error.tsx) and [`app/not-found.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/not-found.tsx)

Verification:
- `npm run build` passes
- `pages/` source files are gone
- The only remaining `pages` path is `.next/server/pages`, which is build output

One note:
- Next updated [`tsconfig.json`](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) to include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts` during type-checking. I left that change in place because the current Next canary expects it.

If you want, I can also do a second pass to tighten the route typings further and clean up the remaining CSS into App Router-specific component styling.
